museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: A quieter archive drawer: the N64 settles, PlayStation keeps widening, and E3 approaches as the place where the rest of 1997 will be arranged.
museum.curatorNote: May is deliberately contextual. Rather than inventing a false release calendar, this exhibit treats it as a preview and retail month between the N64 launch and the June E3/Mario Kart moment.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: May is the intake of breath before summer previews start to land.

items.title: E3 anticipation starts to shape the year
items.body: The Atlanta E3 show was still weeks away, but the press cycle was already leaning toward what would be shown next: PlayStation spectacle, N64 exclusives, and PC hardware pressure.
items.sourceNote: E3 1997 took place in Atlanta from June 19-21; May is build-up context.

items.title: The N64 library question grows louder
items.body: After the March European launch, UK owners could admire Mario 64 and still wonder how quickly the cartridge shelf would fill.
items.sourceNote: Based on N64 European launch timing and early-library context.

items.title: PC boxes make hardware feel unstable
items.body: CD-ROM games increasingly read like compatibility tests. Processor, RAM, DirectX, and graphics acceleration were becoming part of the buying ritual.
items.sourceNote: 3dfx/Glide and late-1997 PC shooter context support the broader hardware trend.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: May's exhibit shelf is more about sustained play than a single safe day-one headline.

items.title: Super Mario 64
items.body: Still the UK N64's museum centrepiece, carrying the machine through its early months.
items.sourceNote: Not a May release; included as active UK shelf context.

items.title: Diablo
items.body: A PC fixture by now, still shaping how players thought about online identity and repeatable loot.
items.sourceNote: January release; included as ongoing momentum.

items.title: Final Fantasy VII
items.body: Still a Japanese release only, but Western magazines were turning it into a coming event.
items.sourceNote: Japan January; Western releases later in 1997.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: The console war feels less like a slogan and more like shelf mechanics.

items.title: PlayStation CD-ROM abundance
items.body: The PlayStation's strength was not one object. It was the amount of plastic on the shelf, the demo discs, and the lower cost of experiments.

items.title: N64 cartridge scarcity
items.body: The N64 felt premium and focused. That made each purchase more deliberate, especially for younger players negotiating with parents.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: May magazines were less verdict than weather report.

items.title: Pre-E3 preview culture
items.headline: What the summer shows might reveal
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed magazine-style cover image standing in for May 1997.
items.curatorNote: The premium magazine voice made waiting feel analytical rather than merely impatient.
items.body: The premium magazine voice made waiting feel analytical rather than merely impatient.

items.title: Demo-disc habit
items.headline: Playable samplers as monthly ritual
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed demo-disc magazine-style cover image standing in for May 1997.
items.curatorNote: A demo disc was the opposite of a rumour: a preview you could actually load.
items.body: A demo disc was the opposite of a rumour: a preview you could actually load.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: The web existed, but the monthly magazine still felt more authoritative.

items.title: Preview news travelled slowly
items.body: Rumours could appear online, but for many UK players the confirmed version arrived later in print.

items.title: PC players compared machines
items.body: Hardware discussion was becoming part of game discussion, especially for players following shooters and 3D acceleration.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: May was the quiet bit between unboxing and the next wave.

items.title: You learned the shelf
items.body: The PlayStation shelf was wide, the N64 shelf was small and bright, and PC games looked like homework until they loaded.

items.title: Waiting had texture
items.body: Waiting meant rereading previews, circling screenshots, and hoping the next issue had something playable on the disc.

sources.label: E3 1997 date context
sources.note: Used for June 19-21 E3 timing; May entry is build-up context.

sources.label: Nintendo 64 European launch context
sources.note: Used for early UK N64 shelf context.
